In fact, data shows that a functional property of [[neurons]] within [[primate]] [[visual cortex|visual cortices']] are their discernment to orientation.<ref>{{cite journal|last=Tootell|first=RB|author2=Hadjikhani, NK |author3=Vanduffel, W |author4=Liu, AK |author5=Mendola, JD |author6=Sereno, MI |author7= Dale, AM |title=Functional analysis of primary visual cortex (V1) in humans.|journal=Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences of the United States of America|date=Feb 3, 1998|volume=95|issue=3|pages=811–7|pmid=9448245|doi=10.1073/pnas.95.3.811 |pmc=33802|bibcode=1998PNAS...95..811T|doi-access=free}}</ref> In contrast, [[haptic perception|haptic]] orientation judgements are recovered through collaborated patterned stimulations, evidently an indirect source susceptible to interference. Likewise, when size is concerned [[haptic perception|haptic]] information coming from positions of the fingers is more immediate. Visual-size perceptions, alternatively, have to be computed using parameters such as [[angle|slant]] and [[distance]]. Considering this, sensory dominance is a useful instinct to assist with calibration. During sensory immaturity, the more simple and robust information source could be used to tweak the accuracy of the alternate source.<ref name="Gori et al. 2008"/> Follow-up work by Gori et al. (2012) showed that, at all ages, vision-size perceptions are near perfect when viewing objects within the [[haptic perception|haptic]] workspace (i.e. at arm's reach).<ref name="Gori et al. 2012">{{cite journal|last=Gori|first=M|author2=Giuliana, L |author3=Sandini, G |author4= Burr, D |title=Visual size perception and haptic calibration during development.|journal=Developmental Science|date=Nov 2012|volume=15|issue=6|pages=854–62|pmid=23106739|doi=10.1111/j.1467-7687.2012.2012.01183.x}}</ref> However, systematic errors in perception appeared when the object was positioned beyond this zone.<ref>{{cite journal|last=Granrud|first=CE|author2=Schmechel, TT|title=Development of size constancy in children: a test of the proximal mode sensitivity hypothesis.|journal=Perception & Psychophysics|date=Nov 2006|volume=68|issue=8|pages=1372–81|pmid=17378423|doi=10.3758/bf03193736|doi-access=free}}</ref> Children younger than 14 years tend to underestimate object size, whereas adults overestimated. However, if the object was returned to the haptic workspace, those visual biases disappeared.<ref name="Gori et al. 2012"/> These results support the hypothesis that haptic information may educate visual perceptions. If sources are used for cross-calibration they cannot, therefore, be combined (integrated). Maintaining access to individual estimates is a trade-off for extra [[neuroplasticity|plasticity]] over accuracy, which could be beneficial in retrospect to the developing body.<ref name="Gori et al. 2008"/><ref name="Ernst 2008"/>
 
Alternatively, Ernst (2008) advocates that efficient integration initially relies upon establishing correspondence – which sensory signals belong together.<ref name="Ernst 2008"/> Indeed, studies have shown that visuo-haptic integration fails in adults when there is a perceived [[space|spatial]] separation, suggesting sensory information is coming from different targets.<ref>{{cite journal|last=Gepshtein|first=S|author2=Burge, J |author3=Ernst, MO |author4= Banks, MS |title=The combination of vision and touch depends on spatial proximity.|journal=Journal of Vision|date=Dec 28, 2005|volume=5|issue=11|pages=1013–23|pmid=16441199 |doi=10.1167/5.11.7 |pmc=2632311}}</ref> Furthermore, if the separation can be explained, for example viewing an object through a [[mirror]], integration is re-established and can even be optimal.<ref>{{cite journal|last=Helbig|first=HB|author2=Ernst, MO|title=Optimal integration of shape information from vision and touch.|journal=Experimental Brain Research|date=Jun 2007|volume=179|issue=4|pages=595–606|pmid=17225091|doi=10.1007/s00221-006-0814-y|s2cid=12049308}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal|last=Helbig|first=HB|author2=Ernst, MO|title=Knowledge about a common source can promote visual- haptic integration.|journal=Perception|year=2007|volume=36|issue=10|pages=1523–33|pmid=18265835|doi=10.1068/p5851|s2cid=14884284}}</ref> Ernst (2008) suggests that adults can obtain this knowledge from previous experiences to quickly determine which sensory sources depict the same target, but young children could be deficient in this area.<ref name="Ernst 2008"/> Once there is a sufficient bank of experiences, confidence to correctly integrate sensory signals can then be introduced in their behaviour.

Lastly, Nardini et al. (2010) recently hypothesised that young children have optimized their sensory appreciation for speed over accuracy.<ref name="Nardini et al. 2010" /> When information is presented in two forms, children may derive an estimate from the fastest available source, subsequently ignoring the alternate, even if it contains redundant information. Nardini et al. (2010) provides evidence that children's (aged 6 years) response latencies are significantly lower when stimuli are presented in multi-cue over single-cue conditions.<ref name="Nardini et al. 2010" /> Conversely, adults showed no change between these conditions. Indeed, adults display mandatory fusion of signals, therefore they can only ever aim for maximum accuracy.<ref name="Nardini et al. 2010" /><ref name="Hills et al. 2002" /> However, the overall mean latencies for children were not faster than adults, which suggests that speed optimization merely enable them to keep up with the mature pace. Considering the haste of real-world events, this strategy may prove necessary to counteract the general slower processing of children and maintain effective vision-action coupling.<ref>{{cite journal|last=Kail|first=RV|author2=Ferrer, E|title=Processing speed in childhood and adolescence: longitudinal models for examining developmental change.|journal=Child Development|date=Nov–Dec 2007|volume=78|issue=6|pages=1760–70|pmid=17988319|doi=10.1111/j.1467-8624.2007.01088.x}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal|last=Kail|first=R|title=Developmental change in speed of processing during childhood and adolescence.|journal=Psychological Bulletin|date=May 1991|volume=109|issue=3|pages=490–501|pmid=2062981|doi=10.1037/0033-2909.109.3.490}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal|last=Ballard|first=DH|author2=Hayhoe, MM |author3=Pook, PK |author4= Rao, RP |title=Deictic codes for the embodiment of cognition.|journal=The Behavioral and Brain Sciences|date=Dec 1997|volume=20|issue=4|pages=723–42; discussion 743–67|pmid=10097009|doi=10.1017/s0140525x97001611|citeseerx=10.1.1.49.3813|s2cid=1961389 }}</ref> Ultimately the developing sensory system may preferentially adapt for different goals – ''speed and detecting sensory conflicts'' – those typical of objective learning.

The late development of efficient integration has also been investigated from computational point of view.<ref>{{Cite journal|title = Reward Maximization Justifies the Transition from Sensory Selection at Childhood to Sensory Integration at Adulthood|journal = PLOS ONE|pmc = 4110011|pmid = 25058591|volume = 9|issue = 7|doi = 10.1371/journal.pone.0103143|first1 = Pedram|last1 = Daee|first2 = Maryam S.|last2 = Mirian|first3 = Majid Nili|last3 = Ahmadabadi|article-number=e103143|year = 2014|bibcode = 2014PLoSO...9j3143D|doi-access = free}}</ref> Daee et al. (2014) showed that having one dominant sensory source at early age, rather than integrating all sources, facilitates the overall development of cross-modal integrations.

=== Applications ===

==== Prosthesis ====

Prosthetics designers should carefully consider the nature of dimensionality alteration of sensorimotor signaling from and to the [[Central nervous system|CNS]] when designing prosthetic devices. As reported in literatures, neural signaling from the CNS to the motors is organized in a way that the dimensionalities of the signals are gradually increased as you approach the muscles, also called muscle synergies. In the same principal, but in opposite ordering, on the other hand, signals dimensionalities from the sensory receptors are gradually integrated, also called sensory synergies, as they approaches the CNS. This bow tie like signaling formation enables the CNS to process abstract yet valuable information only. Such as process will decrease complexity of the data, handle the noises and guarantee to the CNS the optimum energy consumption. 
Although the current commercially available prosthetic devices mainly focusing in implementing the motor side by simply uses EMG sensors to switch between different activation states of the prosthesis. Very limited works have proposed a system to involve by integrating the sensory side. The integration of tactile sense and proprioception is regarded as essential for implementing the ability to perceive environmental input.<ref>{{cite journal |vauthors=Rincon-Gonzalez L, WarrenJ P | year = 2011 | title = Haptic interaction of touch and proprioception: implications for neuroprosthetics | journal = IEEE Trans. Neural Syst. Rehabil. Eng. | volume = 19 | issue = 5| pages = 490–500 | doi=10.1109/tnsre.2011.2166808| pmid = 21984518 | s2cid = 20575638 }}</ref>

==== Visual rehabilitation ====

Multisensory integration has also been shown to ameliorate visual hemianopia. Through the repeated presentation of multisensory stimuli in the blind hemifield, the ability to respond to purely visual stimuli gradually returns to that hemifield in a central to peripheral manner. These benefits persist even after the explicit multisensory training ceases.<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Jiang|first1=Huai|last2=Stein|first2=Barry E.|last3=McHaffie|first3=John G.|date=2015-05-29|title=Multisensory training reverses midbrain lesion-induced changes and ameliorates haemianopia|journal=Nature Communications|volume=6|page=7263|doi=10.1038/ncomms8263|issn=2041-1723|pmc=6193257|pmid=26021613|bibcode=2015NatCo...6.7263J}}</ref>
